screens.title: Why Can't a Bicycle Climb a Mountain in Top Gear?
screens.content:
You're pedaling up a steep mountain road in your bike's highest gear (the fastest gear). Your legs are burning. Every pedal rotation barely moves you forward.

You shift down to the lowest gear. Suddenly you're climbing effortlessly â€” well, your legs are moving much faster, but the bike is moving uphill with way more power.

What just happened? You changed the **gear ratio**. The same human power, but distributed differently between speed and force.

screens.title: Gears and Gear Ratios
screens.content:
A gear is a wheel with teeth. When two gears mesh (touch), they transfer power from one to the other.

If Gear A has 20 teeth and Gear B has 40 teeth, and you spin Gear A once, Gear B only spins halfway. But Gear B spins with twice the force.

**Gear Ratio = Teeth on output gear / Teeth on input gear**

For that mountain bike:
- **Top gear (high ratio):** Input spins 4 times for every 1 output spin. You pedal fast, but the wheel doesn't move as fast relative to your pedal power.
- **Low gear (low ratio):** Input spins 0.5 times for every 1 output spin. You pedal slowly, but the wheel has tons of torque (twisting force).

There's a trade-off:
- **High gear ratio â†’ High torque, low speed** (great for climbing, starting, heavy loads)
- **Low gear ratio â†’ Low torque, high speed** (great for going fast, light loads)

screens.title: Meshing and Torque Transfer
screens.content:
When two gears mesh, the teeth push against each other. The force transfers, but the direction and magnitude change based on the size difference.

Imagine a tiny gear (10 teeth) driving a huge gear (100 teeth):
- The tiny gear spins 10 times for every 1 spin of the big gear
- But the big gear has 10x the torque
- Useful for lifting heavy objects (like a robot arm)

Now imagine a huge gear (100 teeth) driving a tiny gear (10 teeth):
- The tiny gear spins 10 times for every 1 spin of the big gear
- But the tiny gear has 1/10th the torque
- Useful for high-speed motion (like a drill spinning)

Robots use gear trains (multiple gears in sequence) to get the exact ratio they need. A robot that needs to move slowly but with crushing force (like a excavator) uses a high gear ratio. A robot that needs to move quickly with light force (like a picking robot) uses a low ratio.

screens.title: Adjust the Gear Ratio
screens.content: Change the number of teeth on the output gear and watch how speed and torque change. The motor always provides the same power.

screens.title: Robot Arm Design
screens.content:
You're designing a robot arm to assemble iPhones. The motor spins at 1000 RPM and can provide 1 Nm of torque.

**Requirements:**
- The gripper must move slowly (so it doesn't damage delicate parts)
- It needs moderate force (enough to hold the part, not crush it)

**Solution:** A gear ratio of 10:1
- Output speed: 1000 / 10 = 100 RPM (nice and slow)
- Output torque: 1 Ă— 10 = 10 Nm (plenty of force)

If you used a 1:1 ratio, the gripper would move at 1000 RPM â€” too fast to be precise. If you used a 100:1 ratio, you'd have crushing force but could barely move at all.

screens.title: Choose the Gear Ratio
screens.content:
Your robot needs to lift a 50 kg load at the end of its arm. The motor has a maximum torque of 5 Nm. The load is 1 meter from the pivot point (so it needs 50 Nm of torque: 50 kg Ă— 10 m/sÂ˛ Ă— 1 m).

What minimum gear ratio do you need? Use the gear simulator to find out.

params.hintShow: Divide the required torque by the motor torque. That's your minimum gear ratio.

question.prompt: You're designing a robot that needs to move slowly but with high force. Should you use a high or low gear ratio?

options.text: A high gear ratio (input gear much smaller than output gear)
options.feedback: Correct! A high ratio like 10:1 means the output spins slowly but with much more torque. Perfect for strong, slow movement.

options.text: A low gear ratio (input gear similar size to output gear)
options.feedback: A low ratio like 1:1 would give you fast output speed with little torque. That's the opposite of what you want.

options.text: It doesn't matter; the motor will adjust
options.feedback: The motor's speed and torque are fixed. The gearbox changes how those are distributed to the output.
While multiple stages can work, the question asks about high vs. low ratio. You want a high ratio for slow, powerful movement.
